title: NZAVS Sankey Network for Retention
contents:
Contents
Alluvial Graphs
Overall graph of measured and retained
Graph of survival aggregated by waves
Graph of survival aggregated by ethnicity and wave
Graph of survival aggregated by gender and ethnicity
Graph of survival of gender (only)
Graph of survival of Cohort and Wave
Graph of survival: subject-level by waves


First we import the data, and create an indicator for measured/lost/withdrawn. Below is some data wrangling.


Show code

# read data
df <- readRDS(here::here("data", "df"))

# year measured codes, 1 = measured, 0 = not measured, -1 = withdrawn or died
dat <- df %>%
  select(YearMeasured, Wave, Id, EthnicCats, GenCohort, Male) %>%
  mutate(Wave = as.numeric(as.character(Wave))) %>%
  group_by(Id) %>%
  mutate(first = {
    YearMeasured == 1
  } %>% {
    . * !duplicated(.)
  }) %>%
  mutate(
    value_tmp = if_else(first == 1, Wave, NA_real_),
    firstwave  = mean(value_tmp, na.rm = TRUE) # this is a  hack (but works)
  ) %>%
  mutate(state  = ifelse(
    YearMeasured == -1,
    "withdrawn",
    ifelse(
      YearMeasured == 0 & Wave < firstwave,
      "notyetmeasured",
      ifelse(YearMeasured == 0 &
               Wave > firstwave, "lost",
             "measured")
    )
  )) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Wave = as.factor(Wave)) %>% # return Wave to a factor
  dplyr::select(Wave, Id, state, GenCohort, Male, EthnicCats) %>%
  dplyr::filter(state != "notyetmeasured") %>%
  droplevels()

# fill missing values in dataset (when people are not measured all other indicators are "NA")

datt <- dat %>%
  group_by(Id) %>%
  fill(GenCohort, Male, EthnicCats) # fill is a useful function.

Show code

$ EthnicCats <fct> Euro, Euro, Euro, Euro, Euro, Euro, Eur…

How many participants were measured?


Show code

length( unique( datt$Id ) )


[1] 67635

Alluvial Graphs
Next we get the data in to shape for our graphs.
This is just some data wrangling


Show code

# summarise measurement states by waves
dats <- datt %>%
  group_by(Wave, state) %>%
  summarise(n = n())

# check if data is OK for ggalluvial
is_alluvia_form(as.data.frame(dats), axes = 1:2, silent = TRUE)


[1] TRUE

Overall graph of measured and retained


Show code

ggplot(data = dats,
       aes(x = Wave, y = n, alluvium = state)) +
  geom_alluvium(aes(fill = state, colour = state),
                alpha = .75,
                decreasing = FALSE) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "qual", palette = "Set3") +
  scale_color_brewer(type = "qual", palette = "Set3") +
  ggtitle("") + theme_classic()




Graph of survival aggregated by waves


Show code

ggplot(as.data.frame(dats),
       aes(y = n, axis1 = Wave, axis2 = state)) +
  geom_alluvium(aes(fill = state), width = 1 / 12) +
  geom_stratum(width = 1 / 12,
               fill = "black",
               color = "grey") +
  geom_label(stat = "stratum", aes(label = after_stat(stratum))) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Wave", "State"),
                   expand = c(.05, .05)) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank()
  ) +
  ylab("") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "qual", palette = "Set1") +
  ggtitle("NZAVS alluvial graph. wave-level: measured, lost, withdrawn")




Graph of survival aggregated by ethnicity and wave
I think we probably want to remove the “Wave” column. This seems cluttered


Show code


# prepare data
dats2 <- datt %>%
  group_by(Wave) %>%
  count(EthnicCats, state)

# graph
ggplot(as.data.frame(dats2),
       aes(
         y = n,
         axis1 = Wave,
         axis2 = EthnicCats,
         axis3 = state
       )) +
  geom_alluvium(aes(fill = state)) +
  geom_stratum() +
  geom_text(stat = "stratum", aes(label = after_stat(stratum))) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Wave", "EthnicCats", "State"),
                   expand = c(.2, .05)) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank()
  ) +
  ylab("") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "qual", palette = "Set1") +
  ggtitle("NZAVS alluvial graph: ethnicity")

The illustrates another approach, in which we colour code by the predictor not by the state.


Show code

# prepare data

  group_by(Wave,GenCohort) %>%
  count(state)

# dats4

#graph

       aes(
         y = n,
         axis1 = Wave,
         axis2 = GenCohort,

  geom_stratum() +
  geom_text(stat = "stratum", aes(label = after_stat(stratum))) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Wave", "GenCohort","state"),

    axis.ticks = element_blank()

  ylab("") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "qual", palette = "Set1") +
  ggtitle("NZAVS alluvial graph: Cohort x Wave") + theme_classic()




Here we can focus on ethnicity by wave


Show code

#graph
dats2 <- datt %>%
  group_by(Wave,EthnicCats) %>%
  count(state)


ggplot(as.data.frame(dats2),
       aes(
         y = n,
         axis1 = Wave,
         axis2 = EthnicCats,
         axis3 = state
       )) +
  geom_alluvium(aes(fill = EthnicCats)) +
  geom_stratum() +
  geom_text(stat = "stratum", aes(label = after_stat(stratum))) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Wave", "EthnicCats","state"),
                   expand = c(.2, .05)) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank()
  ) +
  ylab("") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "qual", palette = "Set1") +
  ggtitle("NZAVS alluvial graph: Ethnic Categories X Wave") + theme_classic()




Because the ethnic proportions do not change much by time, we can simplify the graph


Show code

dats2s <- datt %>%
  group_by(EthnicCats) %>%
  count(state)


ggplot(as.data.frame(dats2s),
       aes(
         y = n,
         axis1 = EthnicCats,
         axis2 = state
       )) +
  geom_alluvium(aes(fill = EthnicCats)) +
  geom_stratum() +
  geom_text(stat = "stratum", aes(label = after_stat(stratum))) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("EthnicCats","state"),
                   expand = c(.2, .05)) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank()
  ) +
  ylab("") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "qual", palette = "Set1") +
  ggtitle("NZAVS alluvial graph: Ethnic Categories") + theme_classic()




Graph of survival: subject-level by waves
With an individual level graph, I don’t think we have have more another variable (aside from wave).


Show code

# prepare data
datsSS <- dat %>%
  group_by(Wave, state, Id) %>%
  summarise(n = n())

ggplot(datsSS,
       aes(
         x = Wave,
         stratum = state,
         alluvium = Id,
         y = n,
         fill = state,
         label = state
       )) +
  scale_x_discrete(expand = c(.1, .1)) +
  geom_flow() +
  geom_stratum(alpha = .5) +
  geom_text(stat = "stratum", size = 3) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  ggtitle("NZAVS alluvial graph: within-subject x wave") +
  theme_classic()

title: Measuring the intensity and diversity of ethnic identity reveals weakness in standard categorical measurements
contents:
Contents
Introduction
Descriptive Analysis
Import data
Data wrangling
Intensity in categorical identification
Diversity (or spread) of sample ethnic identifications
Super-identification
Under-identification
Proportional diversity
Multiple group identification characteristics
A few observations about multiple-membership

Statistical models
Latent profile analysis of ethnic identifications
Bayesian model for intensity in categorical identification
Bayesian model for super-identification
Bayesian model for diversity
Bayesian model for intensity in total identification

Conclusions
Findings
Limitations
Importance

Appendix 1: estimating causal effects.
Appendix 2. LPA model evaluation
Results
Fit with 7 classes

Appendix 3. Partial correlations (for no good reason)
Appendix 4. Bayesian model of categorical intensity identification under different assumptions

Introduction
The New Zealand national census asks people to respond to the following question:

Which ethnic group(s) do you belong to?

A tick box with the following options appears:
New Zealand European
Māori
Samoan
Cook Island Māori
Tongan
Niuean
Chinese
Indian
Other such as DUTCH, JAPANESE, TOKELAUAN. Please state: [OPEN BOX]
The rate of multiple group identification among those who tick Maori is 45% source
The rate of multiple group identification among those who tick European is 84.1% source
A clear limitation of this approach is we cannot determine the intensity of these ethnicity ratings.
That is, despite the evidence for multiple ethnic identifications among the majority of New Zealander, commonplace methods for measuring ethnicity do not clarify levels of individual ethnic commitments.
Here, we develop a novel measurement instrument and employ this instrument in a sub-sample of the New Zealand population. Although the sample is not reflective of the general New Zealand population, our task here is to clarify that the intensity of commitment enables estimation of fundamental dimensions of ethnic identification that elude commonplace measures of ethnicity, such as those found in national censuses.
Note: possible further readings;:
here here
Descriptive Analysis
Import data


Show code

#import data
df<-read.csv(here::here("data_raw","data_emapaccept_210614.csv"))

# check format
#str(df)



Data wrangling


Show code

# select variables
library(tidyverse)

dat <- df %>%
  select(
    CASE,
    ET06_01_NZEuropean,
    ET06_02_Maori,
    ET06_03_Pasifica,
    ET06_04_Chinese,
    ET06_05_Indian,
    ET06_06_African,
    ET06_07_NA,
    ET06_08_SA,
    ET06_09_European,
    ET06_11_EA,
    ET06_12_SA,
    ET06_13_SeA,
    ET06_14_WA,
    DE02_Gender_Identify_Most,
    Age,
    DE04_Highest_Ed
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    id = as.factor(CASE),
    nzeuro = as.integer(ET06_01_NZEuropean - 1),
    maori = as.integer(ET06_02_Maori - 1),
    pacific = as.integer(ET06_03_Pasifica - 1),
    chinese  = as.integer(ET06_04_Chinese - 1) ,
    indian  = as.integer(ET06_05_Indian - 1),
    african = as.integer(ET06_06_African - 1),
    missing = as.integer(ET06_07_NA - 1),
    euro = as.integer(ET06_09_European - 1),
    ea =  as.integer(ET06_11_EA - 1) ,
    # check with Hedwig what these mean
    sa08 = as.integer(ET06_08_SA - 1),
    # check with Hedwig what these mean
    se12 = as.integer(ET06_12_SA - 1),
    # check with Hedwig what these mean
    sea = as.integer(ET06_13_SeA - 1),
    # check with Hedwig what these mean
    wa = as.integer(ET06_14_WA - 1),
    # check with Hedwig what these mean
    gender_id = as.factor(DE02_Gender_Identify_Most),
    # male or female?
    age = as.numeric(Age),
    edu = DE04_Highest_Ed
  ) %>%
  na_if(-9) %>%  # from Hedwig's notes
  select(
    id,
    gender_id,
    age,
    edu,
    nzeuro,
    maori,
    pacific,
    chinese,
    indian,
    african,
    missing,
    euro,
    ea,
    sa08,
    se12,
    sea,
    wa
  ) 
glimpse(dat)

# create total response and diversity variables 
dt_1 <- dt %>%
  mutate(total = sum(
    c(nzeuro,
      maori,
      pacific,
      chinese,
      indian,
      african,
      missing,
      euro,
      ea,
      sa08,
      se12,
      sea,
      wa
    )
  ),
  diversity = 
  sum(
    c(nzeuro,
      maori,
      pacific,
      chinese,
      indian,
      african,
      missing,
      euro,
      ea,
      sa08,
      se12,
      sea,
      wa
    ) != 0
  )) %>%
  group_by(id) %>%
  mutate(max_ethnic = max(
    c(
      nzeuro,
      maori,
      pacific,
      chinese,
      indian,
      african,
      missing,
      euro,
      ea,
      sa08,
      se12,
      sea,
      wa
    )
  )) %>%
  mutate(
    proportion_maori = as.numeric(maori / total),
    proportion_nzeuro = as.numeric(nzeuro / total),
    proportion_pacific = as.numeric(pacific / total),
    proportion_indian = as.numeric(indian / total),
    proportion_chinese = as.numeric(chinese / total)
  ) %>%
  mutate(superidentified = as.integer(ifelse(total >= 120, 1, 0))) %>% # this is arbitrary
  mutate(underidentified = as.integer(ifelse(total <=80, 1, 0))) %>% # this is arbitrary
  ungroup()

# looking for maximum identity



Below we calculate maximum-ethnic identity groups for each participant:


Show code

nm1 <- c(
  "nzeuro",
  "maori",
  "pacific",
  "chinese",
  "indian",
  "african",
  "missing",
  "euro",
  "ea",
  "sa08",
  "se12",
  "sea",
  "wa"
)



dtt <- dt_1 %>%
  mutate(mak = pmax(!!!rlang::syms(nm1)))

#nrow(dtt)


dtt <- dtt %>%
  mutate(max_eth = ifelse(
    maori == mak & nzeuro != mak,
    "maori_max",
    ifelse(
      nzeuro == mak &  maori != mak,
      "nzeuro_max",
      ifelse(
        indian == mak & nzeuro != mak,
        "indian_max",
        ifelse(
          pacific == mak & nzeuro != mak,
          "pacific_max",
          ifelse (
            chinese == mak & nzeuro != mak,
            "chinese_max",
            ifelse(
              maori == mak &  nzeuro == mak,
              "maori_euro_max",
              ifelse(
                pacific == mak & nzeuro == mak,
                "pacific_euro_max",
                ifelse(
                  indian == mak & nzeuro == mak,
                  "indian_euro_max",
                  ifelse(chinese == mak &
                           nzeuro == mak, "chinese_euro_max", "others")
                )
              )
            )
          )
        )
      )
    )
  ))

Statistical models
Latent profile analysis of ethnic identifications
Here we perform a latent profile analysis to extract ethnic identity types:


Show code

## Try this with counts 
outt <- dt_1 %>%
  dplyr::select(chinese,
                indian,
                nzeuro,
                maori,
                nzeuro,
                pacific)

p_outt <- outt %>%
  single_imputation() %>%
  tidyLPA::estimate_profiles(7)
p_outt %>%
  plot_profiles(add_line = TRUE)




We find that those who are high in Māori identification are also high in NZeuro identification. We also recover a group of middle-level Maori identifiers who are middle identified in NZeuro ethnicity. The highly identified Pacific group is expected to express some NZeuro identification.
The following is a table for the proportion of the sample that fits with these profiles.


Show code

d_out <- tidyLPA::get_data(p_outt) ## 

d_out %>%
  dplyr::select(Class) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(class = as.factor(Class)) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(class) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(n = n()) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(freq = n / sum(n)) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(desc(n)) %>%
  kbl(digits = 2,caption = "proportion of sample in LPA lasses") %>%
  kable_classic_2(c("striped", "hover"), full_width = F) %>%

The third class (green/ NZ euros) and the yellow class (not fitting into any of these categories) are the proportionally largest classes with about 50% of the sample fitting into one of these two categories (25% each). The other classifications describe about 10% of the sample each, with the exception of the 5th classification, which tracks the pacific identifiers at just under 5%.
Again, because of the specific sampling approach in this study, we cannot generalise from this sample to features of the general New Zealand population, but only genearlise to features of the population from which this sample was drawn.
See Appendix 2 for fit criteria in LPA model selection
Bayesian model for intensity in categorical identification
Expected probability for intensity in categorical identification ignoring maximum ethnic identification
Let’s try a Zero 1 inflated probability model as described by Matti Vuorre here
See Appendix 4 for the rational for using this model
Further data wrangling


Show code

# rescale date to 0-1
dt_2z <- dt_2 %>%
  mutate(ethnicity_rating100 = (ethnicity_rating / 100))



Model:


Show code

zoib_model <- bf(ethnicity_rating100 ~ eth_id  + (1 | id),
                 phi ~ eth_id,
                 zoi ~ eth_id,
                 coi ~ eth_id,
                 family = zero_one_inflated_beta())

# fit
fit_zoib  <- brm(
  formula = zoib_model,
  data = dt_2z,
  file = here::here("models", "hedwig-brm-zoib")
)
#prior_summary(fit_zoib)
par_z<- parameters::model_parameters(fit_zoib)
par_z

To understand the expected outcomes we transform the estimates to the probability scale


Show code

library(kableExtra)

head(posterior_samples(fit_zoib, pars = "b_")[,1:4] )%>% 
  mutate_at(c("b_phi_Intercept"), exp) %>%

  kable_classic_2(c("striped", "hover"), full_width = TRUE) %>%
  collapse_rows()

Conclusions
Findings
The NZ Census reveals that people in NZ exhibit multiple ethnic identities. However, the intensity of ethnic commitments remains unclear.
Here, using a continuous scale, we obtain ratings of ethnic identification and use these rating to investigate the intensity and distribution (diversity) of ethnical identities in a sample of 181 study that required ethnic identity ratings. We observe:
the expected maximum ethnic identification in the population from which our sample is drawn is:\(\dots\)
the expected total identification summing across the sample ethnic identification is:\(\dots\)
the expected probability of super-identification (> 120% identified) for the sample population is:\(\dots\)
the expected probability of under-identification (< 80% identified) for the sample population is:\(\dots\)
Focusing on ethnic groups, where an ethnic group is defined as the group for which one has maximum ethnic identification we find:\(\dots\)
the expected maximum ethnic identification per group is:\(\dots\)
the expected total ethnic identification per group is:\(\dots\)
the expected probability of super-identification (> 120% identified) per group is:\(\dots\)
the expected probability of under-identification (< 80% identified) per group is\(\dots\)
the expected probability of diversity of identification (total counts of ethnic categories) is:
Limitations
We caution that the sample used here is not representative of the New Zealand population. We do no extrapolate to features of this society. This sample is not a representative cross section of the New Zealand population. We cannot infer about the prevelance and diversity of ethnic identifications among New Zealanders.
Both cross-cutural variation and longitudinal trends remain unclear. Whether the features we have describe here generalise to other cultures, or whether these features are changing in New Zealand remains unclear.
Before speculating about the the importance of our results, the behavioral implications of high, low, and multiple ethnic identifications remains uncertain. Similarly, whether people are perceived differently because they have strong or weak, homogenous or diverse ethnic identifications remains to be unclear. Future research should investigate the practical implications of the categories our measures have identified.
Importance
We wish to highlight the following key points:
The NZ Zealand Census, and other national censuses use tick boxes and open ended responses to measure ethnic identification. Although this allows for an estimation of the diversity of ethnic identifications, how much people are identified with the ethnic groups to which they identify remains unclear. For example, in New Zealand, although 85% of those who tick “NZ European” also tick another ethnic category, it is unclear how much people in this group maximally identify as NZ European, and how many strongly identify with other groups as well. Our novel method for measuring ethnic identification allows us to estimate these qualities.
In doing so, we identify new sub-populations of jointly highly identified Maori and NZ European identified New Zealanders. This observation challenges simplistic conceptions of bi-culturalism as a phenomenon of difference.
We also track groups of people for whom the sum of ethnicity scores is less than 100. Those who are maximally Māori are, in absolute terms, the least likely group to present under-identification. Those who identify as maximally Indian and those who identify as maximally Chinese tend to have greater probability of expressing under-identification. NZ Europeans sit somwehere in-between.
Appendix 1: estimating causal effects.
Which variables do we need to include as co-variates to estimate causal effects? That depends on our theory of the causal underpinnings of maximum-group identification on total identification, and dispersion counts.
We can explore different theories using Directed Acyclical Graphs.
Here is one possible model. We assume unmeasured effects on the causes of maximum ethnic identification (“U”):


Show code

###   Causal graph. What might affect identifcation? 

library(ggdag)
dg <- dagify(
  total ~ age + max_eth + gender + diversity + edu,
  diversity ~ age + max_eth + gender + diversity + edu,
  max_eth ~ U,
  latent = "U",
  exposure =  "max_eth",
  outcome =   "diversity" ,
  labels = c(
    "total" = "total",
    "age" = "age",
    "max_eth" = "max ethnic\ncategory",
    "gender" = "gender",
    "diversity" = "diversity",
    "edu" = "education",
    "U" = "Unmeasured"
  )
) %>%
  tidy_dagitty() 

dg %>%
  ggdag(use_labels = "label",
        text = FALSE) + theme_dag_blank()




Note that on this DAG, diversity and total are potential collider-confounds:


Show code

ggdag::ggdag_collider(dg,
                      use_labels = "label",
                      text = FALSE) + theme_dag_blank() +
  labs(title = "Colliders")




What is our adjustment set?


Show code

ggdag::ggdag_adjustment_set(
  dg,
  exposure  = "max_eth",
  outcome  = "diversity",
  use_labels = "label",
  text = F
) + theme_dag_blank() +
  labs(title = "Diversity adjustment set")




Also the assumptions of this DAG, we can estimate the effect of max_ethnic identity on Totals


Show code

ggdag::ggdag_adjustment_set(
  dg,
  exposure  = "max_eth",
  outcome  = "total",
  use_labels = "label",
  text = F
) + theme_dag_blank() +
  labs(title = "Diversity adjustment set")

library("tidyLPA")

  single_imputation() %>%
  tidyLPA::estimate_profiles(5:p)

However we need a stronger prior. Uncertainty in the NZeuro rating takes us out of the range of possiblity, with 100 being a maximum response value for each ethnicity.

    fit_tnb,

Finally lets constrain our negative binomial model
First, how to consider the shape parameter for the overdispersion?
This is the default BRMS prior that we used above (see Kurz book )


Show code

library(ggthemes)
library(tidyverse)
ggplot(data = tibble(x = seq(from = 0, to = 60, by = .1)),
       aes(x = x)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = 0, 
                  ymax = dgamma(x, 0.01, 0.01)),
              color = "transparent", 
              fill = canva_pal("Green fields")(4)[2])+
  scale_x_continuous(NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  ggtitle(expression(paste("Our brms default ", gamma, "(0.01, 0.01) prior"))) +
  theme_hc() +
  theme(plot.background = element_rect(fill = "grey92"))




How would it look if we changed it? We allow less disbursion with dgamma(x, 0.2, 0.02)


Show code

library(ggthemes)
library(tidyverse)
ggplot(data = tibble(x = seq(from = 0, to = 500, by = .1)),
       aes(x = x)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = 0, 
                  ymax = dgamma(x, 0.2, 0.02)),
              color = "transparent", 
              fill = canva_pal("Green fields")(4)[2])+
  scale_x_continuous(NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  ggtitle(expression(paste("Our brms default ", gamma, "(0.01, 0.01) prior"))) +
  theme_hc() +
  theme(plot.background = element_rect(fill = "grey92"))




Lets refit, and also keep the intercept

  brm(

    prior = c( prior(gamma(0.2, 0.02), class = shape)),
    family = "negbinomial",
    file = here::here("models", "hedwig-total-nb-b")

par_m_nb2

title: Covariates of Interest for Voice-Pitch Authority Study
description: New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study: year = 2018-2019, N = 47,951
contents:
Contents
Data summary
Sample correlations
Appendix 1
Appendix 2 Causal model
Partial correlations
Notes


Paul and I have recently started on a project looking at associations of voice pitch related to so-called ‘biological codes’, i.e. supposed biological links with high and low pitch related to size, sex and how these are used in mammalian communication. The idea of these codes is that these links influence linguistic and paralinguistic meanings of pitch through associations with, e.g. ‘dominance’=low pitch and ‘submission’=high pitch. We want to test these associations in general, and also the extent to which they are influenced by the attitudes of different listeners, particularly gender attitudes related to normative ideas of gender along the lines of dominant=male, submissive=female.


We’ve been looking around at different gender surveys to try to measure these, and finding parts of different surveys which we think would work in a NZ context, but not one that seems to fit the bill.


We were wondering about the gender-related questions in the NZAVS - which have the advantage of being well tested in the NZ context. We were hoping for your help as part of the project team, with the following info which doesn’t seem to be published (that we’ve found): is it possible to see the variance in the responses to the gender and social dominance related questions in the NZAVS data (e.g. from the latest round)? is it possible to see how the responses vary by age and participant gender for each of these questions? is it possible to see how correlated the responses are to these questions and participant age/gender? We think this would be useful to know better how likely responses to these questions would be to show sufficient spread in gender attitudes for our participants.


If you had any other thoughts on gender surveys in general we’d also be happy to hear 🙂

Data summary
Descriptive summary for variables relevant to inferring the relationshp between vocal pitch and perceived dominance. Note we make “male” a numeric indicator to assess the frequency of males in the sample, with not-male encompassing women and others who identify other than male.

  select(Age, male, SDO, Edu,hostile_sexism, benevolent_sexism, anti_gay)  %>%
  skimr::skim()

Sample correlations
First we prepare the data:


Show code

dt <- dat %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
   age_z = scale(Age),
  #  male = as.numeric(Male) - 1,
    sdo_z = scale(SDO),
    bs_z = scale(benevolent_sexism),
    hs_z = scale(hostile_sexism),
    anti_gay_z = scale(anti_gay)
  ) %>%
  select(age_z, sdo_z, bs_z, hs_z, anti_gay_z)



Here are the raw correlations


Show code

library(correlation)
dt %>%
  correlation(partial = FALSE,
              multilevel = FALSE) %>%
  summary()


# Correlation Matrix (pearson-method)

Parameter | anti_gay_z |    hs_z |    bs_z |   sdo_z
----------------------------------------------------
age_z     |    0.17*** | 0.04*** | 0.07*** | 0.10***
sdo_z     |    0.33*** | 0.48*** | 0.26*** |        
bs_z      |    0.29*** | 0.40*** |         |        
hs_z      |    0.34*** |         |         |        

p-value adjustment method: Holm (1979)

Here are the partial correlations
Graph for the (unadjusted) correlations:


Show code

library(correlation)
library(ggraph)

dt %>%
  correlation(partial = FALSE, multilevel = FALSE ) %>%
  plot()




The indicators here look to be moderately correlated. Indeed a single component accounts for half the variance in these indicators.


Show code

 ddt <- dt%>%
  select(bs_z, hs_z, sdo_z, anti_gay_z) %>%
  drop_na()

# see: https://easystats.github.io/parameters/
parameters::principal_components(ddt)


# Loadings from Principal Component Analysis (no rotation)

Variable   | PC1  | Complexity
------------------------------
bs_z       | 0.67 |     1     
hs_z       | 0.80 |     1     
sdo_z      | 0.73 |     1     
anti_gay_z | 0.66 |     1     

The unique principal component accounted for 51.51% of the total variance of the original data.

We can estimate the extent to which age, male, and education predict these indictators.
Begin male and being older predicts greater sdo, benevolent sexism, hostile sexism, and anti-homosexual attitudes. Education predicts lower prejudice.


Show code

# get data in shape
dt2 <- dat %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    age_z = scale(Age),
    #  male = as.numeric(Male) - 1,
    sdo_z = scale(SDO),
    bs_z = scale(benevolent_sexism),
    hs_z = scale(hostile_sexism),
    anti_gay_z = scale(anti_gay),
    edu_z = scale(edu),
    male_f = Male
  ) %>%
  select(age_z, sdo_z, bs_z, hs_z, anti_gay_z, edu_z, age_z, male_f)

# prepare model
bf1 <- bf(sdo_z ~ age_z + edu_z + male_f, family = "gaussian")
bf2 <- bf(bs_z ~ age_z + edu_z + male_f, family = "gaussian")
bf3 <- bf(hs_z ~ age_z + edu_z + male_f, family = "gaussian")
bf4 <- bf(anti_gay_z ~ age_z + edu_z + male_f, family = "gaussian")

# fit model, we do not set the rescor to TRUE because this will take days to run
fit <- brm(
  bf1 + bf2 + bf3 + bf4 +  set_rescor(rescor = FALSE),
  # allow estimation of residual correlations
  data = dt2,
  file = here::here("models", "voical-pitch-cor-outcomes")
)

mcmc_plot(fit,  type = "areas")



Show code

parameters::model_parameters(fit)


# Fixed effects antigayz

Parameter   | Median |         89% CI |   pd |  Rhat |      ESS
---------------------------------------------------------------
(Intercept) |  -0.12 | [-0.13, -0.11] | 100% | 1.000 |  9429.00
age_z       |   0.14 | [ 0.14,  0.15] | 100% | 0.999 | 10292.00
edu_z       |  -0.13 | [-0.14, -0.12] | 100% | 1.000 | 10742.00
male_fMale  |   0.29 | [ 0.28,  0.31] | 100% | 1.000 |  7606.00

# Fixed effects sigma antigayz

Parameter | Median |       89% CI |   pd |  Rhat |     ESS
----------------------------------------------------------
sigma     |   0.96 | [0.95, 0.96] | 100% | 0.999 | 9968.00

# Fixed effects bsz

Parameter   | Median |         89% CI |   pd |  Rhat |      ESS
---------------------------------------------------------------
(Intercept) |  -0.12 | [-0.13, -0.11] | 100% | 0.999 |  8372.00
age_z       |   0.03 | [ 0.03,  0.04] | 100% | 0.999 |  7519.00
edu_z       |  -0.25 | [-0.25, -0.24] | 100% | 1.000 | 10212.00
male_fMale  |   0.29 | [ 0.27,  0.30] | 100% | 0.999 |  7774.00

# Fixed effects sigma bsz

Parameter | Median |       89% CI |   pd |  Rhat |      ESS
-----------------------------------------------------------
sigma     |   0.94 | [0.94, 0.95] | 100% | 0.999 | 10644.00

# Fixed effects hsz

Parameter   |    Median |         89% CI |     pd |  Rhat |      ESS
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(Intercept) |     -0.21 | [-0.22, -0.20] |   100% | 0.999 |  9550.00
age_z       | -1.67e-03 | [-0.01,  0.01] | 65.33% | 1.000 | 13180.00
edu_z       |     -0.24 | [-0.25, -0.24] |   100% | 0.999 | 10414.00
male_fMale  |      0.50 | [ 0.48,  0.51] |   100% | 0.999 |  7506.00

# Fixed effects sigma hsz

Parameter | Median |       89% CI |   pd |  Rhat |     ESS
----------------------------------------------------------
sigma     |   0.93 | [0.92, 0.93] | 100% | 1.000 | 9286.00

# Fixed effects sdoz

Parameter   | Median |         89% CI |   pd |  Rhat |      ESS
---------------------------------------------------------------
(Intercept) |  -0.17 | [-0.18, -0.16] | 100% | 1.000 |  8783.00
age_z       |   0.07 | [ 0.06,  0.07] | 100% | 1.000 |  6856.00
edu_z       |  -0.18 | [-0.19, -0.18] | 100% | 0.999 | 10659.00
male_fMale  |   0.40 | [ 0.38,  0.41] | 100% | 1.000 |  7815.00

# Fixed effects sigma sdoz

Parameter | Median |       89% CI |   pd |  Rhat |     ESS
----------------------------------------------------------
sigma     |   0.95 | [0.94, 0.95] | 100% | 0.999 | 8551.00

Appendix 1
Note, there is substantial missingness in the ant-homosexuality indicator.
We can inspect missingness using the naniar package.


Show code

library(naniar)
naniar::vis_miss(dat)




If we were to model the relationships betweeen variables, we would want to multiply impute missing responses.
For now, we press on.
Appendix 2 Causal model
It is a good idea to investigate the causal assumptions of a study using a directed acyclical graph (DAG). For causal inference, we must close all backdoor paths between an exposure of interest and the outcome of interest, and we must not introduce any collider confounds.
Assume the following model for potential causal influences, where the exposure variable of interest is vocal pitch and the outcome variable of interest is perceived dominance. We have theoretical reasons to assume that age, male, benevolent sexism , hostile sexism, and social dominance orientation (SDO) can moderate how pitch affects perceived dominance.
To make things harder on ourselves, let’s assume there is a latent variable, called "nastiness’, which gives rise to the correlations we just observed.


Show code

library(ggdag)
dg <- dagify(
  dom ~ pitch + age + male + bs + hs + sdo + U,
  hs ~  age + male + U,
  bs ~ age + male + U,
  sdo ~ age  + male + U,
  pitch ~ age + male + hs + bs + sdo + biology,
  latent = "U",
  exposure =  "pitch",
  outcome =   "dom",
  labels = c(
    "dom" = "dominance",
    "pitch" = "pitch",
    "age" = "age",
    "male" = "male",
    "biology" = "biological endowment",
    "hs" = "hostile sexism",
    "bs" = "benevolent sexism",
    "sdo" = "Soc Dom Orientation",
    "U" = "Nastiness"
  )
) %>%
  tidy_dagitty() 

dg %>%
  ggdag(use_labels = "label", text = FALSE) + theme_dag_blank()




Note that on this DAG, there are many potential collider confounds


Show code

ggdag::ggdag_collider(dg, use_labels = "label", text = FALSE) + theme_dag_blank() +
 labs(title = "Colliders")




It therefore makes sense to assess which adjustements are needed to obtain an un-baised causal inference:


Show code

ggdag::ggdag_adjustment_set(dg, use_labels = "label", text = FALSE) + theme_dag_blank() +
  labs(title = "")




On the assumptions of this DAG, you would be correct to condition on age, benevolent sexism, hostile sexism, male gender and social dominance orientation.
Partial correlations


Show code

dt %>%
  correlation(partial = TRUE,
              multilevel = FALSE) %>%
  summary()


# Correlation Matrix (pearson-method)

Parameter | anti_gay_z |     hs_z |    bs_z |   sdo_z
-----------------------------------------------------
age_z     |    0.14*** | -0.05*** | 0.03*** | 0.06***
sdo_z     |    0.17*** |  0.38*** | 0.06*** |        
bs_z      |    0.15*** |  0.29*** |         |        
hs_z      |    0.17*** |          |         |        

p-value adjustment method: Holm (1979)

Notes
For more information about the NZAVS see here and here

title: Hidden neighbourhood effects may introduce confounding in regression models
contents:
Contents
Causal inference when we include neighbourhood effects.
Simple causal effects



Show code
# Set up

# packages
# install_rethinking
# function for installing dependencies
# ipak <- function(pkg){
# new.pkg <- pkg[!(pkg %in% installed.packages()[, "Package"])]
# if (length(new.pkg)) 
#     install.packages(new.pkg, dependencies = TRUE) 
# sapply(pkg, require, character.only = TRUE)
# }
# # usage
# packages <- c("coda", "plyr", "mvtnorm", "scales", "dagitty")
# ipak(packages)
# next install rethinking
if (!require(rethinking)) {
  devtools::install_github("rmcelreath/rethinking")
}
#next install ggdag
if (!require(ggdag)) {
  devtools::install_github("malcolmbarrett/ggdag")
}


# libraries
library("tidyverse")
library("ggdag")
library("brms")
library("rstan")
library("rstanarm")
library("tidybayes")
library("bayesplot")
library("easystats")
# rstan options
rstan_options(auto_write=TRUE)
options(mc.cores=parallel::detectCores ())
theme_set(theme_classic())



Show code
# options
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  layout = "l-body-outset",
  fig.width = 12,
  fig.height = 10,
  collapse = TRUE,
  R.options = list(width = 60)
)

Imagine a word in which we have the following relationships:


Show code
library(ggdag)
# code for creating a DAG
ggdag_ov <- dagify(Y ~ X + M,
                   M ~ X + U,
                   exposure = "X",
                   outcome = "Y") %>%
  control_for("M")

# plot the DAG
ggdag_ov %>%
  ggdag() + theme_dag_blank()


Suppose that we want to write a mediation model in which we partition out the direct effect of X\(\to\)Y and total effect of X\(\to\)Y through M. However there is an unmeasured neighbourhood effect of U on M. Note that neighbourhood is a collider.


Show code
# find colliders
ggdag::ggdag_collider( ggdag_ov )  + theme_dag_blank()


Because of this, neighbourhood effects can lead to confounding, making it appear as though the effect of X on Y is negative when we condition on M.
To show this, we simulate some data:
Note that in our simulation, X has a positive effect on Y. However, M is a collider, and including M in our model makes it appear as if X is negatively related to Y, even though in our simulation there is no relationship.
We can write a mediation model that does not adjust for neighbourhood effects (U), imagining these neighbourhood effects are unmeasured (as in our current dataset).


Show code
bmlm::mlm_path_plot(xlab = "Focal\n(X)",
              mlab = "Mediator\n(M)",
              ylab = "Outcome\n(Y)")




Show code
path_m <- brms::bf(M ~ X)
path_y <- brms::bf(Y ~ X + M)

m1 <- brms::brm(
  path_m + path_y + set_rescor(FALSE),
  data = d,
  file = here::here("models", "mediation-confound-simulation")
)

# output
# parameters::model_parameters(m1)

For the mediation model we recover the indirect, direct and total effects as follow:


Show code
# get posterior distributions
post <- brms::posterior_samples(m1)

# sum and multiply the posterior distributions to obtain parameter estimates
post2 <- post %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    a = b_M_X,
    b = b_Y_M,
    cp = b_Y_X,
    me = a * b,
    c = cp + me#,
    # pme = me / c
  )

The direct effect (cp) appears to be negative!


Show code
# plot the results
mcmc_intervals(post2) 


But recall, we simulated a direct effect coefficient of 0. This seemingly negative effect of X on Y is the result of conditioning on a collider, which opens a path between X and U.


Show code
ggdag::ggdag_dseparated(ggdag_ov, controlling_for = "M", collider_lines = TRUE )  + theme_dag_blank()


Causal inference when we include neighbourhood effects.
This time, let’s included the previously un-modelled effect of neighbourhoods.


Show code
path_m <- brms::bf(M ~ X )
path_y <- brms::bf(Y ~ X + M + U)

m2 <- brms::brm(
  path_m + path_y + set_rescor(FALSE),
  data = d,
  file = here::here("models", "mediation-confound-simulation-2")
)

# output
# parameters::model_parameters(m2)

Graphing results in a model that adjusts for neighbourhoods effects we correct for the confounding


Show code
# get posterior distributions
post2 <- brms::posterior_samples(m2)

# sum and multiply the posterior distributions to obtain parameter estimates
post2.2 <- post2 %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    a = b_M_X,
    b = b_Y_M,
    cp = b_Y_X,
    me = a * b,
    c = cp + me#,
    # pme = me / c
  )

# plot the results
mcmc_intervals(post2.2) 


That is, we recover the simulated direct effect (namely 0)
In short, if we are modelling relationships in which covariates are affected by neighbourhood effects, we might confound our results.
Simple causal effects
The goods news is that for models in which we are only interested in the total effect of X on Y, it is sufficient for us to condition only X. (All backdoor paths are closed)


Show code
# code for creating a DAG
ggdag_ov2 <- dagify(Y ~ X + M,
                   M ~ X + U,
                   exposure = "X",
                   outcome = "Y")

ggdag::ggdag_adjustment_set(ggdag_ov2)


Doing so gives us X’s total effect, which arises through X’s effect on M.


Show code
par <- lm( Y ~ X, data = d) 

tab_par <- parameters::model_parameters(par) 

tab_par
Parameter   | Coefficient |   SE |        95% CI | t(98) |     p
----------------------------------------------------------------
(Intercept) |       -0.03 | 0.40 | [-0.83, 0.77] | -0.08 | 0.937
X           |        1.30 | 0.43 | [ 0.44, 2.16] |  3.01 | 0.003

Show code
tab_par %>%
  plot()


However, in such a model, the mechanism for the total effect of X on Y would remain obscure. And we should not be misled into thinking that X directly causes Y, even if this model contains no other covariates.

title: Trust in the New Zealand Police has been increasing since 2017
description: However willingness to enage with the police, and trust in politicians, have been decreasing: evidence from the New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study (N = 11,481)

title: How did the Christchurch Mosque Attacks Affect Attitudes to Muslims in New Zealand?
contents:

Talk
Summary
Introduction
Findings
Importance of findings

Appendix 1: Design
Appendix 2: Background
Sources of anti-Muslim prejudice

Appendix 3: Prejudice trends from 2012-2020 reveal a longstanding “Muslim Acceptance Gap”
Appendix 4: Political conservatives also grew more accepting of Muslims after the 2019 Mosque attacks.
Appendix 5: Regression Discontinuity Analysis

df <- readRDS(here::here("data", "df"))

dt <- df %>%
  dplyr::filter(YearMeasured == 1)  %>%
  dplyr::filter(
    Wave == 2019 |
      Wave == 2018 |
      Wave == 2017 |
      Wave == 2016 |
      Wave == 2015 |
      Wave == 2014 |
      Wave == 2013 |
      Wave == 2012
  ) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(Id) %>% filter(n() > 2) %>%
  dplyr::ungroup(Id)  %>%

  dplyr::filter(!is.na(Warm.Muslims)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(

    days = as.integer(TSCORE),
    yr_0 = ((days - min(days)) / 365),
    yrs = (days / 365),
    yr_a = 3545 / 365,

## models below

# muslims

             (1 | Id) ,
           data = km2)


p1 <- ggeffects::ggpredict(m1,
                           terms = "Pre_PostATTACK")

p1p <- plot(p1,
            add.data = TRUE,
            dot.alpha = .05,
            jitter = .8) +
  ylab("Warmth to Muslims (1-7)") + labs(title = "Warm Muslims") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 4.395, color = "green") +
  # scale_y_continuous(limits = c(1, 7)) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d() + xlab("Attack timeline") +
  # geom_vline(xintercept = c(2.8), colour = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  theme_classic()

## For intro

            add.data = TRUE,
            dot.alpha = .05,
            jitter = .8) +

Summary


Show code

p1pp




Figure 1:  Change in acceptance of Muslims following the Christchurch Mosque attacks March 15, 2019. Green horizonal line indicates peak acceptance of Muslims, which occurred following the attacks (N = 11272, Years = 2017-2020, Source = New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study)



Following the Christchurch Mosque attacks, warmth towards Muslim grew.
Despite this growth, warmth to Muslims lags behind all other groups (except overweight people.)
Following the mosque attacks non-Pacific/Māori minority groups with Muslims also received a boost in warmth.
The Muslim acceptance gap remains large.
Introduction
We investigate warmth to Muslims before and after the 15 March 2019 mosque attacks in a nationally diverse longitudinal sample of New Zealanders (N = 11,272), and clarify how levels of warmth changed to Muslims and other groups.1

The NZAVS measures tolerance using validated “affective thermometers” See (Sibley et al. 2020).
Findings
Following the attacks there was a substantial increase in warmth to Muslims (Figure 2a)
Following the attacks, there was also a small boost in warmth to Immigrants (Figure 2b), Asians (Figure 2c), Indians (Figure 2d), Chinese people (Figure 2e), and Refugees (Figure 2f.)
The boost in warmth was specific to Muslims and non-Pacific minorities (Figure 2a-f.)
Despite the post-attack boost in warmth to Muslims, warmth to Muslims still lags behind warmth to other groups. (Figure 2a-f).
Whether the in boost to warmth to Muslims and ethnic groups associated with Muslims persists remains unclear.
Muslims and groups non-Pacific ethnic minorities

Figure 3:  There is no evidence for substantial change in prejudice pre-post shootings among groups that are not associated with Muslims. Green horizonal line indicates peak acceptance of Muslims, which occurred following the attacks (N = 11272, Years = 2017-2020, Source = New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study)



Importance of findings
Although the purpose of the Mosque attacks was to incite division, the attacks caused a strong boost in acceptance.
The reasons for the boost in Muslim acceptance are unclear.

Speculating, the government’s response, media’s response (Shaver et al. 2017), and non-Muslim church responses (Shaver et al. 2016) likely played a role in boosting Muslim acceptance.

Whether growth in acceptance will be sustained remains unclear
Whether acceptance reverts to pre-shooting levels remains unclear.

Figure 4:  Within-participant repeated measures covering the years before and following the attacks allow precise estimates of attitudinal change within-people (N = 11,481).



Appendix 2: Background
New Zealand ranks among the world’s most tolerant societies (OECD 2011). However, Muslims are New Zealand’s least accepted minority group (Shaver et al. 2016, 2017; Hawi et al. 2019, 2019; Yogeeswaran et al. 2019; Greaves et al. 2020; Sibley et al. 2020; Highland et al. 2019).
In the context of New Zealand history, the relatively high levels of prejudice against Muslim, as well as the confusion of Muslims with Arabs, might seem surprising.
First, New Zealand’s cultural diversity exceeds that of Australia, the United Kingdom, France, Germany, the Netherlands, and Scandinavia. Muslims are only a small part of this diversity. In 2018, New Zealand’s Muslim, just over 1.5% of the total population. The Islamic community has been present in New Zealand since at least 1874 (Shepard, 2006). The New Zealand Muslim community is of diverse ethnic and cultural origins — of those that affiliated with Islam in 2013, 26.9% were born in Asia, 25.7% in New Zealand, 23.3% in the Middle East or Africa, and 21% in the Pacific Islands (Center, 2013). Thus, fewer than a quarter of New Zealand Muslims can be classified as ‘Arab.’

Colonial New Zealand state never adopted an official state religion, while principles of religious equality and the promotion of social harmony led to the formation of a secular state primary education system beginning in 1877. In such a setting where religious conflicts are few, anti-religious prejudice of any kind is anomalous.
Another reason that anti-Muslim in New Zealand is puzzling is that New Zealand is one of the world’s most socially progressive and tolerant societies (OECD 2011), which has a long tradition of religious tolerance and accommodation of religious diversity.
A final source of puzzlement about anti-Muslim prejudice in New Zealand is that there is no record of any extremist Muslim group in New Zealand, nor have there been any reported attacks of Muslims on non-Muslims By contrast many attacks against the Muslim minority were reported prior to 15 March 2019, when 51 Muslims were murdered at two mosques in a single day. Members of Islamic communities in New Zealand have long reported verbal and physical harassment, vandalism, negative media portrayal, and/or have faced difficulties in the labour market @. Thus, while Muslims in New Zealand have a basis for concern about their experiences of anti-Muslim prejudice, there is no basis from in experience for anti-Muslim prejudice among the non-Muslim majority
Sources of anti-Muslim prejudice
In previous research, we found that levels of media exposure predicted lower warmth towards Muslims; notably, this effect held at both the low and high end of the political conservativism scale (Shaver et al. 2017). Whether changes in media coverage of Muslims contributed to rising Muslim acceptance following the attacks is a question that our future research will consider.
We also found that prior to the attacks, religiously identified New Zealanders were on average more accepting of Muslims (Shaver et al. 2016). Whether this difference held after the attacks is another question that we will consider in future research.
Appendix 3: Prejudice trends from 2012-2020 reveal a longstanding “Muslim Acceptance Gap”
Figure 5 shows the longer term trend warmth towards Muslims, begining in 2012, when the NZAVS first included questions about about attitudes to Muslims. As evident in the figure, despite gradually increasing warmth towards Muslims, the group as a whole has experienced greater prejudice than other groups. Prejudice of any kind, towards Muslims or other groups, should be addressed because prejudice unfairly affects life experiences for those who are the targets of it. Experiences of unfairness conflict with New Zealand’s aspirations to extend manaakitanga, and whanaungatanga to all New Zealanders.
For more analysis of prejudice trends in New Zealand see: (Sibley et al. 2020)

  theme_minimal() + labs("")   + theme(

  ) + scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  geom_vline(xintercept= 9.71, linetype="dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_hline(yintercept= 4.4, color = "green") +
  ylab("Warmth (1-7)") +
  labs(title="Warmth to Indians")

Appendix 4: Political conservatives also grew more accepting of Muslims after the 2019 Mosque attacks.
Generally, greater political conservativism predicts greater minority group prejudice. In absolute terms, however, growth in Muslim acceptance following the shootings was stronger at the conservative end of the scale (see Figure 6.


Show code

lds4 <-  p71 + p10 + p8 + p9 +  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'a') +   plot_layout(ncol = 2,  byrow = FALSE)  +  plot_layout(guides = 'collect')
lds4 




Figure 6:  Warmth to Muslims (y-axis) and other groups by political conservativism (x-axis) pre/post shootings (the solid lines). The shift betweenand the purple/blue lines (pre-attacks) and the yellow/green lines (post-attacks) shows the magnitude of growth in minority group acceptance. Here we plot all expected values of the model as points. The darkness of the areas on the graph reflect densities of responses. This approach allows us to visualise the variance around average responses. Results reveal that the mosque attacks led to a greater increase in warmth towards Muslims among political conservatives. However, this population had more headroom for growth in warmth. Whether this boost in Muslim warmth is sustained remains to be seen: (N = 11272, Years = 2017-2020, Source = New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study)

  )%>%

  ))) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Neighbourhood_Community = SWB.SoC01) %>%

  dplyr::mutate(days = as.integer(TSCORE),
                years = days / 365) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(days_0 = as.integer(days - min(days))) %>%
  dplyr::filter(Muslim == 0)%>%
  dplyr::mutate(Timeline = make_date(year = 2009, month = 6, day = 30)+ TSCORE)

Show code

df_rdd3 <- df %>%
  dplyr::filter(YearMeasured == 1) %>%
    dplyr::filter(Wave == 2016 | Wave == 2017 | Wave == 2018| Wave == 2019) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    TSCORE,
    WSCORE,
    Terrorism.Anxiety,
    KESSLER6sum,
    KESSLER6,
    BELONG,
    PWI,
    SWB.SoC01,
    YearMeasured,
    WSCORE
  ) %>%
  droplevels() %>%
   dplyr::mutate(Pre_Post_attack = as.factor(ifelse(
    TSCORE >= 3545, "Post_Attack", "Pre_Attack"
  ))) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Pre_Post_attack = forcats::fct_relevel(Pre_Post_attack, c("Pre_Attack", "Post_Attack"))) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Timeline = make_date(year = 2009, month = 6, day = 30)+ TSCORE)

# long term k6 periodicity


# TERRORISM ANXIETY
df_rdd4 <- df %>%
  dplyr::filter(YearMeasured == 1) %>%
    dplyr::filter(Wave != 20099) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    TSCORE,
    WSCORE,
    Terrorism.Anxiety,
    KESSLER6sum,
    KESSLER6,
    BELONG,
    PWI,
    SWB.SoC01,
    YearMeasured,
    WSCORE
  ) %>%
  droplevels() %>%
   dplyr::mutate(Pre_Post_attack = as.factor(ifelse(
    TSCORE >= 3545, "Post_Attack", "Pre_Attack"
  ))) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Pre_Post_attack = forcats::fct_relevel(Pre_Post_attack, c("Pre_Attack", "Post_Attack"))) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Timeline = make_date(year = 2009, month = 6, day = 30)+ TSCORE)



#library(table1)  # make pretty tables quickly

Show code

## RDD FULL SAMPLE


np <-
  ggplot(data = df_rdd,
         aes(
           x = Timeline,
           y = KESSLER6,
           group = Pre_Post_attack,
           colour = Pre_Post_attack
         )) + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma")
change_distress <- np +
  geom_point(alpha = .03) +
 # stat_smooth(method =  stats::loess) +
   stat_smooth(method = "gam") +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Regression discontinuity attack shows little change in distress")  +   theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 6),
    legend.title = element_text(color = "Black", size = 8)
  ) +
  ylab("Kessler 6: scale 0-4")  +
  xlab("Timeline: 2018-OCT-16 to 2019-AUG-12")

cd <-
  change_distress + ggtitle("Regression Discontinuity Analysis of Kessler6 Distress Pre/Post Attacks")
cd

    data = df_rdd3,
    aes(
      x = Timeline,
      y = KESSLER6 ,
      group = Pre_Post_attack,
      colour = Pre_Post_attack
    )
  ) + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma")

    legend.title = element_text(color = "Black", size = 8)
  ) +
  ylab("Kessler 6: scale 0-4")  +
  xlab("Timeline:2016-2020")

  change_ta3l + ggtitle("K6 Anxiety 2016-2020")

Figure 1: Sampling from the electoral rol combines with repeated measures within the same people across New Zealand’s social diversity to enable robust inference about stability and change in attitudes and values across New Zealand over time .



About the New Zealand Atitudes and Values Study
The New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study (NZAVS) is a national panel study that Professor Chris G Sibley started in 2009. The team has since grown to over 40 collaborators.
A panel study is a longitudinal study that follows the same individuals over time. A panel study is different from a census, which samples the population as a whole over time, without tracking individuals.
The NZAVS maintains an up-to-date contact list as participants move house, change names, change telephone accounts, and change email addresses. The study’s personalised telephone campaigns, holiday cards, and participant prize draws have enabled the NZAVS to maintain a remarkably high annual sample retention rate of 80%. (Many longitudinal studies achieve only 20% retention.)
Each year, the NZAVS surveys health, personality, relationships, employment, values, beliefs, and habits within the same people. The study has received responses from 61,535 New Zealanders. Its most recent wave (2019/20) received longitudinal responses from 36,524 New Zealanders. This is about 1% of New Zealand’s adult population. The NZAVS is distinct from other national panel studies for the breadth of its survey questions with broad coverage in political and social psychology, health, and religion/spirituality and for its temporal scale, which encompasses 12 longitudinal waves. Time-series data within the same individuals across a diverse population are powerful because they enable a systematic understanding of the complex interplay between human social and psychological processes that occur within people as they respond to consequential events in our lives (“natural experiments”) (2021).
